title: Initialize the Community Site Repository
summary: Turn the prepared community-site folder into a repository on main and store the course identity only inside that repository.

sketchBlocks.title: Turn a folder into a safe, inspectable repository
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A repository records the changes you make to a folder so you can read its history, compare versions, and recover earlier work. Instead of keeping multiple copies of a finished folder (which loses the story of how the files changed), Git keeps a compact chain of snapshots and metadata. That chain is safer and clearer because each snapshot names who made it, when (locally), and which files were part of that snapshot.

What you will learn in this topic:
- How to make the ordinary community-site folder into a repository whose initial branch is main.
- Why the .git directory holds repository metadata and should not be edited directly.
- How to set a repository-local author name and email so commits are attributed inside this repository only.

A good habit: inspect the working tree and staging area with git status before and after each change. This habit helps you confirm what Git will record and avoids accidental snapshots of unrelated files.

sketchBlocks.title: Worked example: What git init creates and why you should not edit it
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example:

Run git init -b main in a folder to create a repository whose initial branch is main. The command creates a hidden .git directory that stores repository metadata. You usually do not open or edit files inside .git; Git manages them for you.

Example commands and annotated output:

```
$ git init -b main
Initialized empty Git repository in /home/you/project/.git/
$ ls -a
.  ..  .git  README.md  index.html
$ cat .git/HEAD
ref: refs/heads/main
```

Explanation:
- git init -b main: creates the .git directory and sets the repository's initial branch name to main. This avoids depending on the system default branch name.
- ls -a shows .git as a hidden folder; .git is not a tracked project file — it's repository metadata.
- .git/HEAD contains a reference line such as ref: refs/heads/main, which tells Git where HEAD points. Reading it helps you confirm the branch name, but you should not edit it manually.

Why this matters: editing files inside .git can corrupt the repository. Use Git commands (git config, git status, git log) to inspect and change repository state instead of editing metadata files directly.

Step by step, read the commands in the order shown. The first line creates the repository metadata that lets Git track this directory. The second line runs `Initialized empty Git repository in /home/you/project/.git/` as the next repository step in the example. The third line runs `ls -a` as the next repository step in the example. The fourth line runs `.  ..  .git  README.md  index.html` as the next repository step in the example. The next line inspects repository metadata without changing it. The next line runs `ref: refs/heads/main` as the next repository step in the example. Together, the lines form one complete example rather than unrelated commands.

sketchBlocks.title: Move work from the working tree into the staging area
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The staging area (the index) is where you prepare a set of changes that will become the next commit. git add copies the current content of files from the working tree into the staging area; git commit then records exactly what is staged.

Short example (conceptual):

```
# working tree: five new files exist but are untracked
$ git status --short
?? README.md
?? index.html
?? about.md
?? LICENSE
?? CONTRIBUTING.md

# stage all five files
$ git add README.md index.html about.md LICENSE CONTRIBUTING.md
$ git status --short
A  README.md
A  index.html
A  about.md
A  LICENSE
A  CONTRIBUTING.md
```

Interpretation: lines that start with A in git status --short mean the file is staged for commit. Lines that start with ?? are untracked and not yet staged. Staging selectively (only some files) helps you make focused commits that document a single logical change instead of mixing unrelated edits.

sketchBlocks.title: Make a meaningful initial commit and verify a clean working tree
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
After staging the files you want, record them with git commit. Commits are the durable snapshots that become your repository history.

A minimal sequence to create a clear, verifiable first commit:

```
# set identity for this repository only
$ git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
$ git config --local user.email learner@zoeskoul.local

# stage files and commit
$ git add README.md index.html about.md LICENSE CONTRIBUTING.md
$ git commit -m "Initial site snapshot"
$ git status --short
# (empty output, meaning the working tree is clean)
$ git log --oneline
<one-line commit id> Initial site snapshot
```

Why verify: after the commit you should see a single commit in the log and a clean working tree. That tells you the snapshot was recorded and no unintended changes remain uncommitted.

quizDraft.title: Why keep local history instead of duplicate folders?
quizDraft.prompt: Which statement best explains why Git history is safer and clearer than keeping multiple copies of a final folder?
options: Keeping copies is safer because each copy is a full backup of the final files.
Git records snapshots with author and change history so you can inspect and recover specific changes without copying folders.
Duplicate folders are simpler because they avoid repository metadata and hidden files.
A single final copy is enough; you don't usually need earlier versions or commit messages.
quizDraft.hint: Think about what history records that duplicate folders do not.

help.concept: Git records a sequence of named snapshots with metadata (who, when, what changed) so you can inspect differences and reasoning; duplicates only keep final states.
help.hint_1: Git stores both the change content and metadata for each commit; duplicates store only final file contents, not the change story.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that emphasizes a readable history and the ability to inspect changes rather than separate final copies.

quizDraft.title: What does .git contain?
quizDraft.prompt: Which answer best describes the purpose of the .git directory inside a project folder?
options: A copy of all project files that you can edit directly as the tracked source files.
Repository metadata and internal bookkeeping (objects, refs, HEAD, config) that Git manages.
A list of remote hosts and personal credentials used to push changes.
Temporary cached build artifacts generated by the project toolchain.
quizDraft.hint: Think about metadata vs. project files.

help.concept: .git stores repository metadata (object database, refs, configuration, and internal bookkeeping) that Git uses to build history and branches.
help.hint_1: .git contains Git's internal data: objects, refs, HEAD, config; it does not contain the project's editable content as tracked files.
help.hint_2: Pick the option that names metadata and internal repository bookkeeping rather than project content.

quizDraft.title: What git status --short shows
quizDraft.prompt: In a repository that has several new untracked files and one file that was just staged, which git status --short entries are correct examples you might see?
options: Lines starting with '?? README.md' for an untracked README.md
Lines starting with 'A README.md' for a staged README.md
Lines starting with 'U README.md' to show an untracked file
Lines starting with 'D README.md' to show a staged new file
quizDraft.hint: Remember git status --short uses two-character prefixes like ?? for untracked and A for added (staged).

help.concept: git status --short uses concise two-character markers: '??' for untracked files, 'A ' for files staged for addition, 'M ' for modified and staged, etc.
help.hint_1: Look for entries that show untracked files with ?? and staged files with A or M in the short status output.
help.hint_2: Select both the marker for untracked files and the marker that indicates a file is staged for commit.

quizDraft.title: Order to create the first commit
quizDraft.prompt: Reorder these steps into the sequence you would follow to create the repository and record the first commit.
tokens: Edit files in the working tree
git init -b main to create repository
correctOrder: git init -b main to create repository
Edit files in the working tree
quizDraft.hint: Initialization must happen before staging, and staging happens before committing.

help.concept: Creating an initial commit requires creating the repository, preparing files in the working tree, staging chosen files, and then committing the staged snapshot.
help.hint_1: Start with creating the repository (git init -b main), then place files in the working tree, then git add to stage, and finally git commit.
help.hint_2: The full correct sequence begins with repository creation, then working-tree edits, then staging, then the commit.

quizDraft.title: Set the initial branch name
quizDraft.prompt: To set the initial branch name explicitly when creating a new repository use: git init -b [blank1]
quizDraft.template: To set the initial branch name explicitly when creating a new repository use: git init -b [blank1]
quizDraft.hint: The course uses a short, conventional initial branch name.

help.concept: git init -b <name> creates a repository whose first branch has the given name instead of relying on the system default.
help.hint_1: The course standard initial branch name is a single short word commonly used today.
help.hint_2: Choose the missing term as the explicit initial branch name used in these lessons.

quizDraft.title: Repository-local author email
quizDraft.prompt: Set the repository-local author email with: git config --local user.email [blank1]
quizDraft.template: Set the repository-local author email with: git config --local user.email [blank1]
quizDraft.hint: Use the deterministic, repository-local email used in these lessons.

help.concept: git config --local sets configuration values that apply only to the repository in the current directory rather than globally.
help.hint_1: The lessons use a deterministic local identity email for reproducible grading.
help.hint_2: Choose the missing term as the repository-local email.

quizDraft.title: Initialize community-site and configure local identity
quizDraft.prompt:
You have an ordinary folder named community-site with five project files. In the terminal, initialize a Git repository whose initial branch is main and set the repository-local author name and email to exactly these values:

user.name: ZoeSkoul Learner
user.email: learner@zoeskoul.local

Type the commands and press Enter. Do not create any commits yet. After your commands the repository should exist on branch main and the local config must show the specified name and email.

The terminal opens inside `community-site/` with the required starting milestone ready. Type each requested terminal command and press Enter.
quizDraft.hint: Read the Git task "Initialize community-site and configure local identity" and identify the repository state you need to create.

help.concept: This Git exercise checks the repository state produced by "Initialize community-site and configure local identity".
help.hint_1: Use git status before and after each small change so you can see what moved between the working tree, staging area, and history.
help.hint_2: Match the requested file names, branch names, and commit purpose before clicking Check Answer.

quizDraft.starterCode:
# Work in the terminal; the starting repository state is ready.
# Type each requested command and press Enter.
quizDraft.instructions:
You have an ordinary folder named community-site with five project files. In the terminal, initialize a Git repository whose initial branch is main and set the repository-local author name and email to exactly these values:

user.name: ZoeSkoul Learner
user.email: learner@zoeskoul.local

Type the commands and press Enter. Do not create any commits yet. After your commands the repository should exist on branch main and the local config must show the specified name and email.

The terminal opens inside `community-site/` with the required starting milestone ready. Type each requested terminal command and press Enter.

starterFiles.content:
# Work in the terminal; the starting repository state is ready.
# Type each requested command and press Enter.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- "community-site"
mkdir -p -- "community-site"
printf '%s\n' 'Cumulative Git practice project' > "community-site/README.md"
git -C "community-site" init -q -b main
git -C "community-site" config user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C "community-site" config user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
git -C "community-site" add -- "README.md"
git -C "community-site" commit -q -m "Start cumulative project"
printf '%s\n' 'Prepared practice change' >> "community-site/README.md"
git -C "community-site" add -- "README.md"

solutionFiles.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- "community-site"
mkdir -p -- "community-site"
printf '%s\n' 'Cumulative Git practice project' > "community-site/README.md"
git -C "community-site" init -q -b main
git -C "community-site" config user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C "community-site" config user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
git -C "community-site" add -- "README.md"
git -C "community-site" commit -q -m "Start cumulative project"
printf '%s\n' 'Prepared practice change' >> "community-site/README.md"
git -C "community-site" add -- "README.md"

quizDraft.title: Commit the initial snapshot and verify
quizDraft.prompt:
Initialize the community-site repository, set the repository-local author name and email to the required values, stage the five project files, and create the first commit with the message "Initial site snapshot". After committing, git log --oneline should show exactly one commit whose message includes Initial site snapshot, and git status --short should show nothing (a clean working tree).

The terminal opens inside `community-site/` with the required starting milestone ready. Type each requested terminal command and press Enter.
